text: The surgeon performs laparoscopic rectopexy for complete rectal prolapse, with ports placed and dissection of the retrorectal space underway.

This is the normal space which we use in total mesorectal excision in case of carcinoma rectum. We have to be very careful while doing dissection in carcinoma rectum. Because due to traction the ureters on either side will be pushed medially. So we have to be very careful each and every time we have to identify the ureter before doing any further cuts.

The surgeon retracts the uterus anteriorly using a percutaneous stitch, revealing the surgical field in a female patient undergoing laparoscopic rectopexy.
The surgeon retracts the uterus anteriorly using a percutaneous stitch, revealing the anatomical structure in the surgical field of a female patient.

The surgeon applies cranial traction to the rectum after adequate dissection, with a polypropylene mesh covering the posterior two-thirds of the rectum, leaving the anterior one-third uncovered to allow for rectal expansion.

The surgeon applies cranial traction to the rectum, revealing a polypropylene mesh covering the posterior two-thirds of the rectum, while leaving the anterior one-third uncovered to allow for rectal expansion.

The surgeon fixes the polypropylene mesh to the sacral promontory using intracorporeal sutures, specifically one-zero proline sutures. Care is taken to avoid the median sacral vessels during this fixation process.

The surgeon fixes the polypropylene mesh to the sacral promontory using intracorporeal sutures with 1-0 Prolene, while carefully avoiding the median sacral vessels. Tackers can also be used for fixation.
The surgeon sutures the lateral end of the polypropylene mesh to the rectum, securing it in place as part of the laparoscopic rectopexy procedure.

The surgeon sutures the lateral end of the mesh to the rectum, with visible consideration of whether to divide the middle rectal ligament to achieve further cranial traction, potentially reducing recurrence risk.

There is always a controversy whether to divide the ligament, middle rectal ligament. If we divide the ligament, we will be able to get further cranial traction to the rectum, the chances of recurrence is a little bit less.
The surgeon avoids taking a through-and-through bite through the rectum during suturing to prevent potential mass infection.
We should not take through and through bite through the rectum. If we take like that, it will lead to mass infection.
The surgeon has wrapped the mesh around the rectum on both sides, leaving the anterior part of the rectum uncovered to allow for expansion.
The surgeon has wrapped the mesh around the lateral sides of the rectum, leaving the anterior part uncovered to allow for expansion.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal flap on either side using continuous 2-0 Vicryl sutures to prevent intestinal herniation or adhesions to the mesh.

The surgeon applies cranial traction to the rectum while fixing the mesh to its lateral aspect, ensuring proper alignment and tension during the rectopexy procedure.
